title: Part I: The Good Citizen
paragraphs: The towers of Zenith aspired above the morning mist; austere towers of steel and cement and limestone, sturdy as cliffs and delicate as silver rods. They were neither citadels nor churches, but frankly and beautifully office-buildings.

The mist took pity on the fretted structures of earlier generations: the Post Office with its capping of deteriorated limestone, the red brick minarets of hulking old houses, factories with stingy and sooted windows, wooden tenements colored like mud. The city was full of such grotesqueries, but the clean towers were thrusting them from the business center, and on the farther hills were shining new houses, homes—they seemed—for laughter and tranquillity.

Over a concrete bridge fled a limousine of long sleek hood and noiseless engine. These two men sat in the limousine and they were heading away from the city toward the new suburb of Floral Heights where their houses were.

George F. Babbitt was forty-six years old now, in April, 1920, and he made nothing in particular, neither butter nor shoes nor poetry, but he was nimble in the calling of selling houses for more than people could afford to pay.

His large head was pink, his brown hair thin and dry. His face was babyish in slumber, absurd and helpless. His cuff-links were silver set with the grill-work of a golfing trophy, and there were streaks of gold in his tie. On his back, as he lay with his coat off, was shown the label of his new Union-made garments.
He was not fat but he was exceedingly well-fed; his cheeks were pads, and the unroughened hand which lay helpless upon the khaki-colored blanket was soft.

He was a Good Citizen. He belonged to the Boosters' Club and the Chamber of Commerce and the Presbyterian Church. He had made the right friends and said the right things at the right times. He went to Zenith's best barber. He drove the latest model car.
He had, he often reflected, been given everything he could want.
He was not happy.

This he did not admit even to himself. It was a vague thing, this unhappiness. In the mornings sometimes, before he was fully awake, he had a dream of a fairy child who waited for him in a secret garden—a dream that was gone the instant he reached for it. Then he would be awake and the alarm clock would ring and his wife Myra would ask if he wanted another blanket, and the day would begin.

The day was always the same. He would have breakfast and read the newspaper and get in his car and drive to his office in the Reeves Building and do real estate things all day and come home and have dinner and read the newspaper again and go to bed.
And it was a good life. He knew it was a good life. He was ashamed of himself for not feeling it.

title: Part II: The Rebellion
paragraphs: The friendship with Paul Riesling was the most real thing in Babbitt's life.

Paul was a small man with a slight stoop and an air of melancholy that Babbitt found inexplicably comforting. He had wanted to be a violinist. He had played beautifully in school, and for a little while it seemed he might actually do it. Then he had married Zilla and gone into the roofing business, and the violin had been put away.

Babbitt and Paul had been friends since college. They went fishing together every summer. On the fishing trips, Babbitt could be something he was not the rest of the year. He did not have to be boosterish or enthusiastic about Zenith or real estate. He could just sit in a boat with Paul and say nothing and feel something he could not name.
"What do you want, Paul?" Babbitt asked one afternoon on the lake.
"I don't know," said Paul. "Just—something different, I suppose. Something real."
"This is real. The lake. The fish."
"Yes. But tomorrow we go home."
They were quiet for a while.
"Do you ever think about—just leaving?" Paul said. "Just going somewhere else and starting over?"
"Where would you go?"
"I don't know. Somewhere they don't know me. Somewhere I don't have to be—" He did not finish.
Babbitt thought about the fairy child in the secret garden. He did not tell Paul about her.
Then Paul did something terrible. He shot Zilla—not fatally, but he shot her—and went to prison. And Babbitt was left without his one real thing.
Something changed in Babbitt after Paul went to prison. He began to drift. He became, in a small way, rebellious.

He befriended a painter named Seneca Doane, who was known in Zenith as a radical and a troublemaker—a man who thought the working classes had legitimate grievances. This was not the kind of friendship a Booster Club member was supposed to have.
He had a brief, anxious affair with a widow named Tanis Judique. It was not romantic. It was the gesture of a man who had everything and wanted something else and did not know what.
The Booster Club began to notice. His friends pulled away. There were hints, then suggestions, then a direct talk from his colleague Vergil Gunch.
"George," said Gunch, "people are talking. You need to be careful."
"About what?" Babbitt said.
"About the kind of people you're running with. About—certain attitudes."

Babbitt looked at him. He wanted to say something. He wanted to say: I am forty-seven years old and I have never done anything real. I have lived in this city all my life and I have built nothing that matters and I am afraid.
He said: "I'll be more careful."

title: Part III: The Return
paragraphs: Myra Babbitt fell ill—a serious illness, a thing that required the doctors and worried the children. And Babbitt, at her bedside, was surprised to find that he cared.

He had not been a good husband. He had not been a bad husband either. He had been a husband—present, adequate, going through the motions. But now she was thin and white against the hospital pillow and he sat beside her and held her hand, and something happened in him.

He came back to Zenith. He came back to the Booster Club and the Chamber of Commerce and the church. He apologized to Vergil Gunch without being asked. He was welcomed back without undue ceremony, because that was how these things were done.
He was a Good Citizen again. He said the right things at the right times and belonged to the right organizations and drove the right car.
And yet.

His son Ted had fallen in love with a girl named Eunice Littlefield and wanted to run away with her and get married. The proper Zenith thing was to be horrified. Babbitt was not horrified. He thought of Paul, and of Tanis, and of the fairy child in the garden. He thought of Paul sitting in prison because he had wanted something different and had not known how to want it without violence.

"Don't be like me," Babbitt told his son. "I've never done a single thing I wanted to in my whole life. If you want to run off with Eunice, run. If you want to be an engineer instead of going into real estate, be an engineer. Don't let them make you into me."
Ted stared at him.
"You aren't—the way I thought you were, Dad," he said.
"I know," said Babbitt. "I'm sorry for it. But it's too late for me to change. It's not too late for you. Don't waste it."

He watched his son go off with the girl, and he felt something that was partly grief and partly—possibly—something like relief. He had done one real thing. He had said one true thing. It was not much. But it was his.
He walked back into the house. Myra was better now; she was sitting up. She needed something from the kitchen, and he went to get it.
He was a Good Citizen of Zenith. He was forty-eight years old. The dream of the fairy child was very faint now, very far away.
But he remembered it.
